file.description: Block metadata. Declares the block name, attributes for toggling roadmap sections, and asset paths. The block is dynamic (uses render.php) so no save markup is stored in post_content — only the attribute state.

Block registration entry point. Imports the Edit component and metadata from block.json. Because this is a dynamic block (server-rendered via render.php), no save function is needed — we return null so WordPress stores only the block delimiter comment and serialized attributes in post_content.

Editor component. Renders a compact, minimal placeholder in the block editor — intentionally NOT the full roadmap. Provides InspectorControls with ToggleControls so the author can choose which roadmap sections to display on the frontend. Uses useBlockProps for proper block selection and focus handling.

Save function — returns null because this is a dynamic block. WordPress stores only the block comment delimiter and serialized attributes in post_content. The frontend markup is produced by render.php on each request.

Shared styles applied on both the frontend and in the editor. Uses BEM naming convention with the root block element "telex-theater-relations". Styles are nested per BEM methodology. Covers the full roadmap layout (TOC, sections, code blocks, diagrams, cards) and responsive breakpoints.

Editor-only styles for the compact placeholder card. These styles are applied only inside the block editor and do not affect the frontend rendering. Uses BEM naming nested under the block's editor-placeholder element.

Frontend interactivity script. Adds collapsible section toggling, smooth-scroll TOC navigation, and IntersectionObserver-based active-link highlighting. Uses plain JavaScript with standard DOM APIs — no frameworks, no Interactivity API.

Server-side render callback for the block. Uses a Singleton-like renderer class to encapsulate section data and HTML generation. Outputs the full roadmap with TOC, collapsible sections, code blocks, comparison tables, diagrams, and implementation phases. Each section can be toggled via block attributes.
file.content:
bool, * 'showCastCrew' => bool, * 'showEnsemble' => bool, * 'showVisualizations' => bool, * 'showScaling' => bool, * 'showImplementation' => bool, * ]; * $content = ''; // Empty for dynamic blocks. * $block = WP_Block; // The parsed block instance. * ``` * * ARCHITECTURAL CONTEXT — FIXED DECISIONS: * ──────────────────────────────────────── * 1. Both "people" and "productions" are POST TYPES, not taxonomies. * 2. The production post type (`gatherpress_play`) is registered by * gatherpress-productions plugin — we consume it, never register it. * 3. We register a new `gatherpress_person` post type with * `gatherpress-shadow-source` support, which auto-creates a * `_gatherpress_person` shadow taxonomy. * 4. All new functionality is gated behind the `gatherpress-relations` * post_type_supports string. Nothing is hard-bound to any post type * slug. Any CPT can opt in by declaring the support. * 5. Consumer post types tag themselves with shadow taxonomy terms to * model relationships — same mechanism as venue ↔ event tagging. * 6. Roles are relationship metadata stored as structured post meta * (`_gatherpress_relations`) on the consumer post. * 7. The pattern is agnostic — it works for any "special post type → * person" connection: theater cast, event organizers, speakers, etc. * 8. Discovery of supporting post types uses WP core's * `get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' )`. * 9. All wiring runs on `init` at priority 100 to ensure every CPT * is already registered. * * @package GatherPressRelations */ if ( ! defined( 'ABSPATH' ) ) { exit; } if ( ! class_exists( 'GatherPress_Relations_Renderer' ) ) { /** * Class GatherPress_Relations_Renderer * * Singleton renderer that produces the complete roadmap HTML. * * WHY Singleton? * If multiple roadmap blocks exist on the same page, each * `require` of render.php would try to re-declare the class. * The `class_exists` guard above prevents that. Internally, * `get_instance()` ensures only one renderer object exists, * avoiding redundant data structures in memory. * * @since 0.1.0 */ class GatherPress_Relations_Renderer { /** * Singleton instance. * * @since 0.1.0 * @var self|null */ private static ?self $instance = null; /** * Returns the single renderer instance. * * @since 0.1.0 * @return self */ public static function get_instance(): self { if ( null === self::$instance ) { self::$instance = new self(); } return self::$instance; } /** * Private constructor — enforces Singleton access. * * @since 0.1.0 */ private function __construct() {} /** * Renders the complete roadmap block HTML. * * HOW the output is structured: * ``` *
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
*
* ``` * * @since 0.1.0 * @param array $attributes Block attributes from the editor. * @return string Complete HTML for the roadmap. */ public function render( array $attributes ): string { $sections = $this->get_sections( $attributes ); ob_start(); ?>
>
render_toc( $sections ); ?>
render_header(); ?> $section ) { echo $this->render_section( $section, $index + 1 ); } ?>
$attributes Block attributes. * @return array Enabled sections. */ private function get_sections( array $attributes ): array { $all_sections = array( array( 'attr_key' => 'showDataModeling', 'id' => 'gpr-section-1', 'title' => __( 'Data Modeling & Architecture', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'content_method' => 'render_data_modeling', ), array( 'attr_key' => 'showCastCrew', 'id' => 'gpr-section-2', 'title' => __( 'Cast & Crew List Block', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'content_method' => 'render_cast_crew', ), array( 'attr_key' => 'showEnsemble', 'id' => 'gpr-section-3', 'title' => __( 'People / Ensemble Blocks', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'content_method' => 'render_ensemble', ), array( 'attr_key' => 'showVisualizations', 'id' => 'gpr-section-4', 'title' => __( 'Smart Data-Relation Visualizations', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'content_method' => 'render_visualizations', ), array( 'attr_key' => 'showScaling', 'id' => 'gpr-section-5', 'title' => __( 'Scaling & Performance', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'content_method' => 'render_scaling', ), array( 'attr_key' => 'showImplementation', 'id' => 'gpr-section-6', 'title' => __( 'Implementation Roadmap', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'content_method' => 'render_implementation', ), ); return array_values( array_filter( $all_sections, function ( array $section ) use ( $attributes ): bool { return ! empty( $attributes[ $section['attr_key'] ] ); } ) ); } /** * Renders the roadmap header with title and subtitle. * * @since 0.1.0 * @return string Header HTML. */ private function render_header(): string { ob_start(); ?>

$sections Enabled sections. * @return string TOC HTML. */ private function render_toc( array $sections ): string { ob_start(); ?>

for the header? * Buttons are natively keyboard-focusable and activatable * with Enter/Space, making the section toggle fully accessible * without manual tabindex or keydown handling. * * @since 0.1.0 * @param array{id: string, title: string, content_method: string} $section Section data. * @param int $number 1-based section index. * @return string Section HTML. */ private function render_section( array $section, int $number ): string { $body_id = str_replace( 'section', 'body', $section['id'] ); $content = $this->{$section['content_method']}(); ob_start(); ?>

  • with show_ui => false, show_admin_column => true, publicly_queryable => true, show_in_rest => true, and rewrite => false.', 'gatherpress-relations' ); ?>
  • false).', 'gatherpress-relations' ); ?>

// STEP 1: Register the "gatherpress_person" post type (the ONLY CPT we register). // The gatherpress_play CPT comes from gatherpress-productions — we never register it. register_post_type( 'gatherpress_person', [ 'label' => __( 'People', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'public' => true, 'supports' => [ 'title', 'editor', 'thumbnail', 'custom-fields', 'gatherpress-shadow-source' ], 'show_in_rest' => true, 'has_archive' => true, 'rewrite' => [ 'slug' => 'person' ], ] ); // STEP 2: Bridge external CPTs into our system. // The gatherpress_play CPT is owned by gatherpress-productions — we add our // support flag to it, not the other way around. add_action( 'init', function (): void { if ( post_type_exists( 'gatherpress_play' ) ) { add_post_type_support( 'gatherpress_play', 'gatherpress-relations' ); } // Any other external CPT can be bridged the same way: // if ( post_type_exists( 'gatherpress_event' ) ) { // add_post_type_support( 'gatherpress_event', 'gatherpress-relations' ); // } }, 15 ); // STEP 3: Dynamic wiring — use get_post_types_by_support() from WP core. // No post type slug appears in this callback. add_action( 'init', function (): void { // get_post_types_by_support() returns an array of post type names // that declare the given support string. This is a core function // (since WP 4.7) that reads from the global $wp_post_types registry. $consumers = get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ); foreach ( $consumers as $pt ) { // Register junction meta per consumer. register_post_meta( $pt, '_gatherpress_relations', [ 'type' => 'string', 'single' => true, 'show_in_rest' => true, 'description' => 'JSON array of person-role assignments.', 'auth_callback' => function (): bool { return current_user_can( 'edit_posts' ); }, ] ); } if ( ! empty( $consumers ) ) { add_filter( 'gatherpress_shadow_taxonomy_object_types', function ( array $types, string $source ) use ( $consumers ): array { if ( 'gatherpress_person' === $source ) { $types = array_merge( $types, $consumers ); } return array_unique( $types ); }, 10, 2 ); } }, 100 ); // Priority 100: safely after ALL CPTs are registered. // The _gatherpress_play shadow taxonomy wiring onto events is handled by // gatherpress-productions itself — not our responsibility.

// Post meta key: _gatherpress_relations // Stored on: gatherpress_play post (or any consumer with gatherpress-relations support) // Value: JSON array of person-role assignments // // Each entry references a person by their shadow term slug // (the underscore-prefixed post_name from the _gatherpress_person taxonomy). // // PAYLOAD EXAMPLE: // post_id: 42 (gatherpress_play: "Hamlet") // meta_key: "_gatherpress_relations" // meta_value: [ { "person_shadow_slug": "_jane-doe", "person_post_id": 15, "role": "Hamlet", "department": "cast", "billing_order": 1 }, { "person_shadow_slug": "_alex-rivera", "person_post_id": 23, "role": "Ophelia", "department": "cast", "billing_order": 2 }, { "person_shadow_slug": "_sam-chen", "person_post_id": 8, "role": "Director", "department": "direction", "billing_order": 1 }, { "person_shadow_slug": "_taylor-wright", "person_post_id": 31, "role": "Lighting Designer", "department": "design", "billing_order": 1 } ] // WHY both shadow_slug and post_id? // The shadow_slug is the canonical link (it survives re-imports), // and the post_id is a fast denormalized lookup for rendering. // If the person post is renamed, the shadow term slug updates // automatically — a scheduled reconciliation job or a // save_post hook can refresh the meta entries.

// Junction meta is registered DYNAMICALLY for every post type that // declares the 'gatherpress-relations' support. // This happens in the init:100 callback (see "How Relationships Are Wired"). // // No post type slug is hard-coded — the same meta key (_gatherpress_relations) // is registered on every supporting post type discovered via // get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ): // // $consumers = get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ); // foreach ( $consumers as $pt ) { // register_post_meta( $pt, '_gatherpress_relations', [ // 'type' => 'string', // JSON-encoded array // 'single' => true, // 'show_in_rest' => true, // 'description' => 'JSON array of {person_shadow_slug, person_post_id, role, department, billing_order}.', // 'auth_callback' => function (): bool { // return current_user_can( 'edit_posts' ); // }, // ] ); // } // // If a conference plugin adds: // add_post_type_support( 'session', 'gatherpress-relations' ); // then 'session' posts automatically get _gatherpress_relations meta, the // _gatherpress_person shadow taxonomy wired onto them, and the Cast & Crew // block works on session pages — zero changes in our plugin code.

┌────────────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ gatherpress_person (CPT) │ │ gatherpress_play (CPT) │ │ supports: shadow-source │ │ supports: shadow-source │ │ │ │ (from gatherpress-productions)│ │ post_id: 15 │ │ post_id: 42 │ │ post_name: jane-doe │ │ post_name: hamlet │ │ post_title: Jane Doe │ │ post_title: Hamlet │ └──────────┬─────────────────────┘ └──────────┬─────────────────────┘ │ │ auto-generates auto-generates │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ ┌────────────────────────────────┐ │ _gatherpress_person │ │ _gatherpress_play │ │ (shadow taxonomy) │ │ (shadow taxonomy) │ │ hidden, show_in_rest │ │ hidden, show_in_rest │ │ │ │ │ │ term: _jane-doe │ │ term: _hamlet │ │ term: _alex-rivera │ │ term: _tempest │ │ term: _sam-chen │ │ term: _midsummer │ └──────────┬─────────────────────┘ └──────────┬─────────────────────┘ │ │ wired onto via filter wired onto via filter │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ gatherpress_play (CPT) │ gatherpress_event (CPT) │ │ │ │ │ Tagged with _gatherpress_person: │ Tagged with _gatherpress_person:│ │ _jane-doe, _sam-chen │ _jane-doe │ │ │ Tagged with _gatherpress_play: │ │ post_meta: _gatherpress_relations│ _hamlet │ │ [{ │ │ │ person_shadow_slug: │ post_meta: _gatherpress_relations│ │ "_jane-doe", │ [{ │ │ role: "Hamlet", │ person_shadow_slug: │ │ department: "cast" │ "_jane-doe", │ │ }] │ role: "Host" │ │ │ }] │ └───────────────────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────────┘

<!-- Show the connected person's details on an event template --> <!-- wp:gatherpress/venue {"sourcePostType":"gatherpress_person"} --> <!-- wp:post-title /--> <!-- wp:post-featured-image /--> <!-- wp:post-content /--> <!-- /wp:gatherpress/venue --> <!-- Show the connected production's details on an event template --> <!-- wp:gatherpress/venue {"sourcePostType":"gatherpress_play"} --> <!-- wp:post-title /--> <!-- /wp:gatherpress/venue -->

import { useSelect } from '@wordpress/data'; import { store as coreStore } from '@wordpress/core-data'; /** * Hook that returns all post types supporting 'gatherpress-relations'. * * HOW this works: * ─────────────── * The @wordpress/core-data store exposes getPostTypes() which fetches * post type objects from the REST API (/wp/v2/types). Each object * includes a `supports` property. We filter client-side to find all * post types that declare 'gatherpress-relations' support. * * WHY the core data store instead of a custom endpoint? * - Zero new REST routes needed. * - The data is already cached by the store across components. * - Stays in sync with the server's post type registry automatically. * * @returns {{ consumerTypes: Object[]|null, isResolving: boolean }} * * PAYLOAD example (consumerTypes): * [ * { slug: 'gatherpress_play', name: 'Productions', supports: { ... } }, * { slug: 'gatherpress_event', name: 'Events', supports: { ... } }, * ] */ function useRelationConsumerTypes() { return useSelect( ( select ) => { const { getPostTypes, isResolving } = select( coreStore ); const allTypes = getPostTypes( { per_page: -1 } ); if ( ! allTypes ) { return { consumerTypes: null, isResolving: true }; } // Filter for post types with gatherpress-relations support. // The REST API exposes supports as an object with boolean values. const consumers = allTypes.filter( ( type ) => type.supports?.[ 'gatherpress-relations' ] ); return { consumerTypes: consumers, isResolving: isResolving( 'getPostTypes', [{ per_page: -1 }] ), }; }, [] ); }

  • —
  • —

<div class="wp-block-gatherpress-cast-crew"> <section class="cast-crew__department"> <h3 class="cast-crew__department-title">Cast</h3> <div class="cast-crew__grid"> <a href="/person/jane-doe/" class="cast-crew__card"> <img class="cast-crew__headshot" src="..." alt="Jane Doe" /> <span class="cast-crew__name">Jane Doe</span> <span class="cast-crew__role">Hamlet</span> </a> </div> </section> <section class="cast-crew__department"> <h3>Direction</h3> </section> </div>

// In render.php of the cast-crew block: $post_id = get_the_ID(); $relations = json_decode( get_post_meta( $post_id, '_gatherpress_relations', true ) ?: '[]', true ); // Group by department. $departments = []; foreach ( $relations as $entry ) { $dept = $entry['department'] ?? 'other'; $departments[ $dept ][] = $entry; } // Sort each department by billing_order. foreach ( $departments as &$members ) { usort( $members, fn( $a, $b ) => ( $a['billing_order'] ?? 99 ) <=> ( $b['billing_order'] ?? 99 ) ); } // Render each department section... foreach ( $departments as $dept_name => $members ) { echo '<section class="cast-crew__department">'; echo '<h3>' . esc_html( ucfirst( $dept_name ) ) . '</h3>'; foreach ( $members as $member ) { // Resolve the source person post from the shadow term slug. // Shadow term slugs are prefixed with underscore: // "_jane-doe" maps to gatherpress_person post with post_name "jane-doe". $person_post_id = $member['person_post_id']; $person_post = get_post( $person_post_id ); $headshot = get_the_post_thumbnail_url( $person_post_id, 'medium' ); $permalink = get_permalink( $person_post_id ); // ... render card with link to person single page } echo '</section>'; }

  • —
  • —
  • —
  • —
  • —

// Departments and their display order. // Filterable so themes/plugins can customise for their domain. // A theater plugin might add "Musicians"; a conference plugin // might replace the whole list with "Speakers", "Moderators", "Staff". $departments_config = apply_filters( 'gatherpress_relations_departments', [ 'cast' => __( 'Cast', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'direction' => __( 'Direction', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'design' => __( 'Design', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'stage_management' => __( 'Stage Management', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'musicians' => __( 'Musicians', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'production' => __( 'Production', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'other' => __( 'Other', 'gatherpress-relations' ), ] );

// block.json attributes for Person Profile Card: { "attributes": { "personPostId": { "type": "number", "default": 0 }, "showRolesHistory": { "type": "boolean", "default": true }, "maxRoles": { "type": "number", "default": 5 } } } // Render logic pseudocode: // // 1. Get person post: get_post( $personPostId ) // 2. Get headshot: get_the_post_thumbnail_url( $personPostId ) // 3. Get bio: get_the_excerpt( $personPostId ) or wp_trim_words() // // 4. Find productions featuring this person using the shadow taxonomy. // The person post "jane-doe" has a shadow term "_jane-doe" in // the _gatherpress_person taxonomy. Query consumer posts tagged // with that term: // // $shadow_slug = '_' . get_post_field( 'post_name', $personPostId ); // $shadow_term = get_term_by( 'slug', $shadow_slug, '_gatherpress_person' ); // // // Query ALL post types that support gatherpress-relations. // $consumer_types = get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ); // // $productions = get_posts( [ // 'post_type' => $consumer_types, // 'posts_per_page' => $maxRoles, // 'tax_query' => [ // [ // 'taxonomy' => '_gatherpress_person', // 'terms' => $shadow_term->term_id, // ], // ], // 'orderby' => 'date', // 'order' => 'DESC', // ] ); // // 5. For each result, extract the role from _gatherpress_relations meta: // $cast = json_decode( get_post_meta( $prod->ID, '_gatherpress_relations', true ), true ); // $entry = array_filter( $cast, fn( $e ) => $e['person_shadow_slug'] === $shadow_slug ); // // 6. Render card HTML.

  • —
  • —
  • —
// Ensemble Grid: query all gatherpress_person posts. $persons = new WP_Query( [ 'post_type' => 'gatherpress_person', 'posts_per_page' => 50, 'orderby' => 'title', 'order' => 'ASC', 'meta_query' => [ [ 'key' => 'ensemble_status', 'value' => 'current' ], ], ] ); // WHY full posts instead of taxonomy terms? // Each person has a featured image (headshot), post_content (bio), // and custom fields — all natively available in the loop. // No term_meta gymnastics needed. // Frontend filtering via view.js: // - Render all cards with data-department and data-season attributes // - JavaScript filters by toggling the hidden attribute // - No additional server requests needed for client-side filtering

// Timeline data structure (assembled in render.php): // For gatherpress_person post_id = 15 ("Jane Doe"): // // 1. Resolve shadow term: slug "_jane-doe" in _gatherpress_person taxonomy // 2. Query all consumer posts tagged with that shadow term using // get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ) for the post_type arg // 3. For each result, extract role from _gatherpress_relations meta // $timeline = [ [ 'year' => '2024', 'production' => 'Hamlet', 'post_id' => 42, 'role' => 'Hamlet', 'department' => 'cast', 'permalink' => '/production/hamlet/', ], [ 'year' => '2023', 'production' => 'A Midsummer Night\'s Dream', 'post_id' => 38, 'role' => 'Titania', 'department' => 'cast', 'permalink' => '/production/midsummer/', ], [ 'year' => '2023', 'production' => 'The Tempest', 'post_id' => 35, 'role' => 'Assistant Director', 'department' => 'direction', 'permalink' => '/production/tempest/', ], ]; // Rendered as a vertical timeline with year markers, // linked production titles, role badges, and department indicators. // Each production card links to the production single page.

// Query: "Find all consumer posts featuring gatherpress_person post_id = 15" // // Step 1: Resolve the person's shadow term in the _gatherpress_person taxonomy. $person_slug = get_post_field( 'post_name', 15 ); // "jane-doe" $shadow_slug = '_' . $person_slug; // "_jane-doe" $shadow_term = get_term_by( 'slug', $shadow_slug, '_gatherpress_person' ); // Step 2: Query ALL consumer post types tagged with that shadow term. // Use get_post_types_by_support() so no slug is hard-coded. $consumer_types = get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ); $results = get_posts( [ 'post_type' => $consumer_types, 'posts_per_page' => 50, 'tax_query' => [ [ 'taxonomy' => '_gatherpress_person', 'terms' => $shadow_term->term_id, ], ], 'orderby' => 'date', 'order' => 'DESC', ] ); // Step 3: For each result, extract the role from _gatherpress_relations meta. foreach ( $results as $post ) { $relations = json_decode( get_post_meta( $post->ID, '_gatherpress_relations', true ) ?: '[]', true ); $person_entry = array_filter( $relations, fn( $e ) => $e['person_shadow_slug'] === $shadow_slug ); $entry = reset( $person_entry ); // $entry['role'] => "Hamlet" // $entry['department'] => "cast" } // WHY this is elegant: // The taxonomy query uses WordPress core's optimised indices. // The role extraction is a simple JSON parse on a small array. // No custom table needed at this scale.

// On gatherpress_play post_id = 42: $relations = json_decode( get_post_meta( 42, '_gatherpress_relations', true ) ?: '[]', true ); foreach ( $relations as $entry ) { // Resolve person post from shadow slug. // Shadow slug "_jane-doe" maps to gatherpress_person post_name "jane-doe". $person_post_id = $entry['person_post_id']; $person_post = get_post( $person_post_id ); $permalink = get_permalink( $person_post_id ); // Count how many consumer posts this person appears in. // Use the shadow term's count — WordPress maintains this automatically! $shadow_term = get_term_by( 'slug', $entry['person_shadow_slug'], '_gatherpress_person' ); $total_appearances = $shadow_term ? $shadow_term->count : 0; // Render: "Jane Doe as Hamlet (12 productions)" // with a link to /person/jane-doe/ }

// Custom REST endpoints for relation queries: // GET /gatherpress-relations/v1/person/15/appearances // Resolves gatherpress_person post 15's shadow term, queries consumer // posts tagged with it (using get_post_types_by_support), enriches // with role data from _gatherpress_relations meta. // Response: [ { "consumer_id": 42, "consumer_type": "gatherpress_play", "title": "Hamlet", "role": "Hamlet", "department": "cast", "date": "2024-03-15", "permalink": "https://example.com/production/hamlet/" } ] // GET /gatherpress-relations/v1/consumer/42/relations // Reads _gatherpress_relations meta, resolves each person post, groups // by department. // Response: { "cast": [ { "person_post_id": 15, "name": "Jane Doe", "role": "Hamlet", "headshot": "https://example.com/wp-content/uploads/...", "permalink": "https://example.com/person/jane-doe/" } ], "direction": [ { "person_post_id": 8, "name": "Sam Chen", "role": "Director", "headshot": "...", "permalink": "https://example.com/person/sam-chen/" } ] } // GET /gatherpress-relations/v1/recommendations?attended=78,92 // Finds shadow terms from attended events, queries upcoming // events sharing those terms. // Response: [ { "event_id": 110, "title": "The Tempest Opening Night", "reason": "Same director: Sam Chen", "date": "2025-01-20", "permalink": "https://example.com/event/tempest-opening/" } ]

use GatherPress\Core\Shadow_Source; // Inside a pre_get_posts callback: $shadow_source = Shadow_Source::get_instance(); $source_post = $shadow_source->resolve_post_from_query_context( $query ); if ( $source_post instanceof WP_Post ) { $tax_query = (array) ( $query->get( 'tax_query' ) ?: [] ); $tax_query[] = $shadow_source->build_tax_query_clause( $source_post ); $query->set( 'tax_query', $tax_query ); } // resolve_post_from_query_context() handles both paths: // - Frontend: uses is_singular() to find the current post // - REST editor preview: reads gatherpress_shadow_source_post_id // and gatherpress_shadow_source_post_type from query attributes // // build_tax_query_clause() returns: // [ // 'taxonomy' => '_gatherpress_person', // (or _gatherpress_play, _gatherpress_venue, etc.) // 'field' => 'term_id', // 'terms' => [ 42 ], // the shadow term ID for this source post // ]

// Cache the "appearances for person X" enriched query result. function gatherpress_get_person_appearances( int $person_post_id ): array { $cache_key = 'gp_person_appearances_' . $person_post_id; $cached = get_transient( $cache_key ); if ( false !== $cached ) { return $cached; } // Resolve shadow term. $shadow_slug = '_' . get_post_field( 'post_name', $person_post_id ); $shadow_term = get_term_by( 'slug', $shadow_slug, '_gatherpress_person' ); if ( ! $shadow_term ) { return []; } // Discover consumer types dynamically. $consumer_types = get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ); // Taxonomy query: fast, uses core's indexed term_relationships. $posts = get_posts( [ 'post_type' => $consumer_types, 'posts_per_page' => 100, 'tax_query' => [ [ 'taxonomy' => '_gatherpress_person', 'terms' => $shadow_term->term_id ], ], ] ); // Enrich with role data from each consumer's meta. $result = []; foreach ( $posts as $post ) { $relations = json_decode( get_post_meta( $post->ID, '_gatherpress_relations', true ) ?: '[]', true ); $entry = array_filter( $relations, fn( $e ) => $e['person_shadow_slug'] === $shadow_slug ); $entry = reset( $entry ); $result[] = [ 'consumer_id' => $post->ID, 'consumer_type' => $post->post_type, 'title' => $post->post_title, 'role' => $entry['role'] ?? '', 'department' => $entry['department'] ?? '', 'date' => $post->post_date, ]; } set_transient( $cache_key, $result, HOUR_IN_SECONDS ); return $result; } // Invalidate on any consumer post save. // When a consumer post is saved, clear caches for every person // tagged on it via the _gatherpress_person shadow taxonomy. // // This hook fires for ALL post types — we check for gatherpress-relations // support before doing any work. add_action( 'save_post', function( int $post_id ): void { $post_type = get_post_type( $post_id ); if ( ! $post_type || ! post_type_supports( $post_type, 'gatherpress-relations' ) ) { return; } $shadow_terms = wp_get_object_terms( $post_id, '_gatherpress_person', [ 'fields' => 'slugs' ] ); foreach ( $shadow_terms as $slug ) { // Shadow slug "_jane-doe" → person post_name "jane-doe" $person_slug = ltrim( $slug, '_' ); $person_posts = get_posts( [ 'post_type' => 'gatherpress_person', 'name' => $person_slug, 'fields' => 'ids', 'posts_per_page' => 1, ] ); if ( ! empty( $person_posts ) ) { delete_transient( 'gp_person_appearances_' . $person_posts[0] ); } } } );

// Custom table schema: // Table: {$wpdb->prefix}gatherpress_relations_lookup // // ┌────────┬─────────────────┬────────────────┬──────────────┬───────────┬───────────────┐ // │ id │ consumer_id │ person_id │ role │ department│ billing_order │ // │ BIGINT │ BIGINT (FK) │ BIGINT (FK) │ VARCHAR(255) │ VARCHAR │ INT │ // ├────────┼─────────────────┼────────────────┼──────────────┼───────────┼───────────────┤ // │ 1 │ 42 (gp_play) │ 15 (gp_person) │ Hamlet │ cast │ 1 │ // │ 2 │ 42 (gp_play) │ 23 (gp_person) │ Ophelia │ cast │ 2 │ // │ 3 │ 78 (gp_event) │ 15 (gp_person) │ Host │ other │ 1 │ // └────────┴─────────────────┴────────────────┴──────────────┴───────────┴───────────────┘ // // Indices: // - PRIMARY KEY (id) // - INDEX (consumer_id) -- "Who's in this show?" // - INDEX (person_id) -- "Where have you seen them?" // - INDEX (department) -- Filter by department // // Query: "All consumer posts for person 15": // SELECT * FROM {table} WHERE person_id = 15 // ORDER BY consumer_id DESC; // → < 1ms even with 10,000 rows. // // WHY "consumer_id" instead of "production_id"? // The lookup table is agnostic — it works for gatherpress_play, // gatherpress_event, sessions, or any consumer post type. The // consumer_id column stores the post ID of whatever post type // the person is connected to.

$cache_key = 'person_appearances_' . $person_post_id; $cache_group = 'gatherpress_relations'; $result = wp_cache_get( $cache_key, $cache_group ); if ( false === $result ) { $result = $wpdb->get_results( $wpdb->prepare( "SELECT * FROM {$wpdb->prefix}gatherpress_relations_lookup WHERE person_id = %d", $person_post_id ) ); wp_cache_set( $cache_key, $result, $cache_group, HOUR_IN_SECONDS ); }

// Register the gatherpress_person post type. // This is the ONLY post type we register — gatherpress_play comes // from gatherpress-productions and we consume it as-is. register_post_type( 'gatherpress_person', [ 'label' => __( 'People', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'labels' => [ 'name' => __( 'People', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'singular_name' => __( 'Person', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'add_new_item' => __( 'Add New Person', 'gatherpress-relations' ), 'edit_item' => __( 'Edit Person', 'gatherpress-relations' ), ], 'public' => true, 'has_archive' => 'people', 'show_in_rest' => true, 'rest_base' => 'people', 'menu_icon' => 'dashicons-groups', 'supports' => [ 'title', 'editor', 'thumbnail', 'excerpt', 'custom-fields', 'gatherpress-shadow-source', // Auto-creates _gatherpress_person shadow taxonomy ], 'rewrite' => [ 'slug' => 'person' ], ] );

/** * Wire shadow taxonomy + junction meta onto every post type * that declares 'gatherpress-relations' support. * * HOW this works: * On 'init' (priority 100, safely after ALL CPTs are registered), * use WP core's get_post_types_by_support() to discover consumer * post types. For each one, register the _gatherpress_relations meta * and collect the slug for shadow taxonomy wiring. * * WHY get_post_types_by_support()? * This is a WP core function (since 4.7) that returns an array of * post type slugs declaring the given support. It's the canonical, * fastest way to discover supporting CPTs without manual iteration. * Signature: get_post_types_by_support( array|string $feature, string $operator = 'and' ): string[] * * WHY priority 100? * Ensures ALL plugins and themes have registered their CPTs before * we scan. GatherPress registers at default priority (10), and * gatherpress-productions may register at a slightly higher priority. * Using 100 is a safe belt-and-suspenders approach. * * WHY post_type_supports instead of a hard-coded list? * - Any third-party plugin can opt its CPT in with one line: * add_post_type_support( 'my_cpt', 'gatherpress-relations' ); * - The wiring layer never mentions 'gatherpress_play' or * 'gatherpress_event' by name — it discovers them at runtime. * - This mirrors GatherPress's own pattern: gatherpress-shadow-source, * gatherpress-venue-information are all post_type_supports strings. */ add_action( 'init', function (): void { $consumers = get_post_types_by_support( 'gatherpress-relations' ); foreach ( $consumers as $pt ) { // Register junction meta per consumer. register_post_meta( $pt, '_gatherpress_relations', [ 'type' => 'string', 'single' => true, 'show_in_rest' => true, 'description' => 'JSON array of person-role assignments.', 'auth_callback' => function (): bool { return current_user_can( 'edit_posts' ); }, ] ); } // Wire the _gatherpress_person shadow taxonomy onto all consumers. if ( ! empty( $consumers ) ) { add_filter( 'gatherpress_shadow_taxonomy_object_types', function ( array $object_types, string $source ) use ( $consumers ): array { if ( 'gatherpress_person' === $source ) { $object_types = array_merge( $object_types, $consumers ); } return array_unique( $object_types ); }, 10, 2 ); } }, 100 ); // Priority 100: safely after ALL CPTs are registered.

/** * Opt the gatherpress_play post type into the relations system. * * WHY add_post_type_support instead of editing the CPT registration? * The gatherpress_play CPT is owned by gatherpress-productions — we * never modify its registration. add_post_type_support() is the * idiomatic way for one plugin to extend another's post type. * * WHY priority 15? * gatherpress-productions registers gatherpress_play at priority 10. * We add support at 15, before our wiring callback at 100 scans * for the support flag via get_post_types_by_support(). * * If gatherpress-productions is not active, post_type_exists() * returns false and we skip — no fatal, no dependency error. */ add_action( 'init', function (): void { if ( post_type_exists( 'gatherpress_play' ) ) { add_post_type_support( 'gatherpress_play', 'gatherpress-relations' ); } }, 15 ); // The same pattern works for events, sessions, or any other // consumer post type — the CPT owner or a bridge plugin adds: // add_post_type_support( 'gatherpress_event', 'gatherpress-relations' ); // and the wiring layer discovers it automatically at init:100.

// In render.php of the cast-crew-list block: $post_id = get_the_ID(); $post_type = get_post_type( $post_id ); // Guard: only render on post types that support the relations system. if ( ! post_type_supports( $post_type, 'gatherpress-relations' ) ) { return '<p>' . esc_html__( 'This post type does not support person-role assignments.', 'gatherpress-relations' ) . '</p>'; } // Read the junction meta — same key on every supporting post type. $relations = json_decode( get_post_meta( $post_id, '_gatherpress_relations', true ) ?: '[]', true ); // Group by department, resolve person posts, render cards... // (identical to Section 2's render logic)

import { useSelect } from '@wordpress/data'; import { store as coreStore } from '@wordpress/core-data'; import { store as editorStore } from '@wordpress/editor'; /** * Hook: check if the current post type supports gatherpress-relations. * * HOW this works: * 1. Read the current post type from the editor store. * 2. Fetch the post type object from the core data store. * 3. Check its `supports` property for 'gatherpress-relations'. * * WHY the core data store? * - The REST API (/wp/v2/types/{slug}) exposes the supports object. * - The store caches it across components — no redundant HTTP requests. * - No custom endpoint needed. * * @returns {{ supportsRelations: boolean, isResolving: boolean }} */ function useSupportsRelations() { return useSelect( ( select ) => { const currentPostType = select( editorStore ).getCurrentPostType(); const postTypeObj = select( coreStore ).getPostType( currentPostType ); if ( ! postTypeObj ) { return { supportsRelations: false, isResolving: true }; } return { supportsRelations: !! postTypeObj.supports?.[ 'gatherpress-relations' ], isResolving: false, }; }, [] ); }

  • register_post_type( 'gatherpress_person', [ 'supports' => [ ..., 'gatherpress-shadow-source' ] ] )
  • add_post_type_support( 'gatherpress_play', 'gatherpress-relations' )
  • add_filter( 'gatherpress_shadow_taxonomy_object_types', ... )

  • single-gatherpress_person.html

  • GET /gatherpress-relations/v1/person/{id}/appearances
  • GET /gatherpress-relations/v1/consumer/{id}/relations

  • add_post_type_support( 'gatherpress_event', 'gatherpress-relations' )

gatherpress-relations/ ├── gatherpress-relations.php # Plugin bootstrap (Singleton) ├── includes/ │ ├── class-post-types.php # gatherpress_person CPT registration ONLY │ │ # (gatherpress_play is external, from │ │ # gatherpress-productions plugin) │ ├── class-supports.php # Defines & discovers post_type_supports: │ │ # - gatherpress-relations │ │ # - (future: gatherpress-ensemble, etc.) │ │ # Uses get_post_types_by_support() on init:100 │ │ # to discover consumers and wire shadow taxonomy │ │ # + _gatherpress_relations meta for each │ ├── class-bridge.php # add_post_type_support() calls that bridge │ │ # our features onto external CPTs: │ │ # - gatherpress_play (gatherpress-productions) │ │ # - gatherpress_event (GatherPress core) │ │ # Each guarded by post_type_exists() │ │ # Fires on init:15 (before wiring at init:100) │ ├── class-meta.php # Post meta registration (_gatherpress_relations, │ │ # ensemble_status, etc.) │ │ # Driven by supports — meta is registered │ │ # per post type from get_post_types_by_support() │ ├── class-rest-endpoints.php # Custom REST routes for relation queries │ │ # /gatherpress-relations/v1/person/{id}/appearances │ │ # /gatherpress-relations/v1/consumer/{id}/relations │ └── class-cache.php # Transient & object cache helpers │ # with save_post invalidation hooks ├── blocks/ │ ├── cast-crew-list/ # Grouped roster block (render.php) │ │ ├── block.json │ │ └── src/ │ │ ├── index.js │ │ ├── edit.js # useEntityProp for _gatherpress_relations meta │ │ │ # uses @wordpress/core-data store for supports │ │ ├── render.php # Shadow term resolution + meta parse │ │ │ # Guards with post_type_supports() check │ │ ├── view.js │ │ ├── style.scss │ │ └── editor.scss │ ├── person-profile-card/ # Single person card │ ├── ensemble-grid/ # Filterable person grid │ ├── person-timeline/ # Chronological production history │ ├── where-seen/ # "Where Have You Seen Them?" │ └── upcoming-for-you/ # GatherPress RSVP recommendations └── templates/ ├── single-gatherpress_person.html # Person detail page # Note: single-gatherpress_play.html is owned by gatherpress-productions. # We do NOT ship a production template — the external plugin owns it. # Our cast-crew-list block is inserted into the production template # by the site editor, not bundled as a default template.
render()? * The Singleton ensures only one GatherPress_Relations_Renderer * object exists, even if multiple roadmap blocks appear on the page. * Each call to render() receives its own $attributes, so each block * instance can show a different subset of sections. */ // phpcs:ignore WordPress.Security.EscapeOutput.OutputNotEscaped -- All output is escaped within the renderer methods. echo GatherPress_Relations_Renderer::get_instance()->render( $attributes );

file.description: Package configuration with build scripts. Dependencies are managed at the workspace level via pnpm.

file.content:
<?php /** * Plugin Name: GatherPress Relations * Description: A comprehensive architectural roadmap block documenting best practices for connecting persons to productions with roles in WordPress — powered by GatherPress shadow taxonomies. * Version: 0.1.0 * Requires at least: 6.4 * Requires PHP: 8.0 * Author: GatherPress * License: GPLv2 or later * License URI: https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-2.0.html * Text Domain: gatherpress-relations * * @package GatherPressRelations */
if ( ! defined( 'ABSPATH' ) ) { exit; // Exit if accessed directly. }
/** * Class GatherPress_Relations_Plugin * * Singleton entry-point for the GatherPress Relations plugin. * * WHY a Singleton? * ──────────────── * WordPress loads every active plugin's main file on every request. * A Singleton guarantees the block is registered exactly once, even if * the file is inadvertently required a second time (e.g. via a * `require_once` in a test harness or another plugin). * * HOW it works: * ───────────── * 1. `get_instance()` is called at the bottom of this file. * 2. On the first call the constructor runs, hooking `register_block` * into the `init` action at the default priority (10). * 3. When WordPress fires `init`, `register_block()` points * `register_block_type()` at the compiled `build/` directory, * which contains the `block.json` manifest and all compiled assets. * 4. Subsequent calls to `get_instance()` return the stored instance * without re-hooking. * * @since 0.1.0 */ if ( ! class_exists( 'GatherPress_Relations_Plugin' ) ) {
class GatherPress_Relations_Plugin {
/** * Holds the single instance of this class. * * WHY nullable static? * Because PHP needs a class-level slot that persists across calls * and starts empty so the first call can populate it. * * @since 0.1.0 * @var self|null */ private static ?self $instance = null;
/** * Returns the singleton instance, creating it on first access. * * HOW callers use this: * ```php * GatherPress_Relations_Plugin::get_instance(); * ``` * * @since 0.1.0 * @return self The single plugin instance. */ public static function get_instance(): self { if ( null === self::$instance ) { self::$instance = new self(); } return self::$instance; }
/** * Constructor — private to enforce Singleton access via get_instance(). * * WHY private? * Prevents external code from calling `new GatherPress_Relations_Plugin()` * and bypassing the single-instance guarantee. * * HOW it initialises: * Hooks `register_block` into `init` so that `register_block_type()` * runs after WordPress has loaded its block-type infrastructure. * * @since 0.1.0 */ private function __construct() { add_action( 'init', array( $this, 'register_block' ) ); }
/** * Registers the block using metadata from the compiled build directory. * * WHY __DIR__ . '/build/'? * `register_block_type()` reads `block.json` from this path to discover * the block name, attributes, scripts, styles, and the `render` callback * (render.php). The `build/` directory is produced by `wp-scripts build` * and contains transpiled JS, compiled CSS, and a copy of block.json. * * PAYLOAD (what register_block_type receives): * ``` * Path: /wp-content/plugins/gatherpress-relations/build/ * Manifest: build/block.json * Assets: build/index.js, build/style-index.css, build/view.js, build/render.php * ``` * * @since 0.1.0 * @return void */ public function register_block(): void { register_block_type( __DIR__ . '/build/' ); } } }
/* * Bootstrap the plugin. * * WHY here and not inside a hook? * The Singleton constructor itself defers work to `init`. * Calling get_instance() at include-time simply registers the hook; * no heavy lifting happens until WordPress fires `init`. */ GatherPress_Relations_Plugin::get_instance();

# GatherPress Relations
**Contributors:** GatherPress **Tags:** block, roadmap, relations, architecture, shadow-taxonomy **Tested up to:** 6.8 **Stable tag:** 0.1.0 **License:** GPLv2 or later **License URI:** <https://www.gnu.org/licenses/gpl-2.0.html>
A comprehensive architectural roadmap block that documents best practices for connecting persons to productions (or any special post type) with roles in WordPress — powered by GatherPress shadow taxonomies, post meta, and relationship patterns that feel close to core.
## Description
This block renders an interactive, navigable roadmap that answers a critical WordPress architecture question: **How do you connect "persons" to "special posts" with given roles, in a way that is close to WP core, provides the best editor experience, is most flexible, and scales long-term?**
The roadmap is built on a fixed architectural decision: **both people and productions are post types with `gatherpress-shadow-source` support**. This auto-generates hidden shadow taxonomies that power many-to-many relationships — the same primitive GatherPress uses for venue ↔ event tagging. The pattern is fully agnostic and works for any combination of source and consumer post types.
### What the roadmap covers
1. **Data Modeling** — Why both people and productions are post types with shadow-source support, how shadow taxonomies model relationships, and how roles become junction metadata stored as post meta on the consumer. 2. **Cast & Crew List Block** — A linked roster showing person → role, grouped by department, powered by shadow taxonomy terms and junction meta. 3. **People / Ensemble Blocks** — Person Profile Card, Ensemble Grid with filters, and Person Production Timeline — all leveraging full post type capabilities (block editor, featured images, templates). 4. **Smart Data-Relation Visualizations** — "Where Have You Seen Them?", "Who's In This Show?", and recommendation-style "Upcoming for You" blocks using shadow taxonomy queries and `Shadow_Source` utility methods. 5. **Scaling Strategies** — Caching layers, denormalized lookup tables, and migration paths. 6. **Implementation Roadmap** — Phased rollout from foundation to GatherPress RSVP integration.
In the editor, the block renders a compact placeholder with the roadmap title and a brief summary. On the frontend, the full roadmap is rendered with collapsible sections, smooth animations, and a sticky table of contents for navigation.
## Installation
1. Upload the plugin files to the `/wp-content/plugins/gatherpress-relations` directory, or install the plugin through the WordPress plugins screen directly. 2. Activate the plugin through the **Plugins** screen in WordPress. 3. Insert the **Shadow Taxonomy Relations Roadmap** block into any post or page via the block editor.
## Frequently Asked Questions
### Can I customize the roadmap content?
The roadmap content is authored as a comprehensive architectural reference. You can toggle the visibility of individual sections via the block's Inspector Controls in the editor sidebar.
### Does this block create any custom post types or taxonomies?
No. This block is purely presentational — it documents the architecture. You would implement the described patterns (post types with `gatherpress-shadow-source` support, shadow taxonomy wiring, junction meta) separately in your theme or plugin.
### Is the roadmap content static or dynamic?
The content is server-rendered from structured data in the render callback. The frontend JavaScript adds interactivity (collapsible sections, smooth scrolling, table of contents highlighting) but does not fetch additional data.
## Screenshots
1. The full roadmap rendered on the frontend with collapsible sections and sticky navigation. 2. The compact editor placeholder showing the roadmap title and section overview.
## Changelog
### 0.1.0
- Initial release with full roadmap content, collapsible sections, and sticky table of contents.
## Architecture
This block is designed as a living architectural document. The patterns described within follow WordPress core conventions, leverage the GatherPress shadow taxonomy primitive, the REST API, and block editor extensibility. While the primary use case is theater ensembles, the architecture is fully agnostic — it applies to event organizers, conference speakers, or any person connected via a specific role to any supporting post type.
